# Chapter 3 — Why the Diagonal Matters

*The longest line on a square is the one that goes corner to corner. Everything begins there.*

There is a way to find out whether someone has folded a lot of paper. Ask them to fold a square in half. Watch where they choose to fold. A beginner folds horizontally — top edge to bottom edge — because that is what folding "in half" looks like. An experienced folder folds along the diagonal. The motion is faster. The grip is firmer. They do not have to think about it.

This is not a stylistic preference. The diagonal does more work than any other fold, and once you understand why, you cannot unsee it.

## The longest path

A square with sides of length one has a diagonal of length √2 — about 1.414. The diagonal is forty-one percent longer than the side. This is not a small advantage. Every fold transfers material from one part of the paper to another, and a longer fold transfers more.

When you fold along the diagonal, you are dividing the square into two right triangles. Those triangles are the starting shape for the bird base, the fish base, the frog base, and the waterbomb base. The four canonical bases of classical origami all begin with the diagonal because the diagonal gives the most material to work with.

## A small experiment

Take a square. Any square. Fold it in half along the horizontal. Unfold. Now fold it in half along the diagonal. Unfold. Compare the two creases.

The horizontal crease is one unit long. The diagonal crease is √2 units long. If you fold along the diagonal again — corner to corner the other way — you get a cross of two diagonals meeting at the center. From this single configuration you can reach roughly half the classical origami models in existence.

Try it with a piece of letter paper. Try it with a napkin. Try it with whatever rectangle is nearest. The principle holds for any quadrilateral with right angles. The longest internal path is the diagonal, and the diagonal is where the form has the most to give.

## What changes when you stop using the diagonal

Modern origami artists sometimes deliberately avoid the diagonal. They fold along irregular angles, on rectangles instead of squares, on polygons with five or seven sides. The work that results is striking — figures with off-center balance, models that twist as they collapse, structures that could not exist in the classical tradition.

But the move away from the diagonal is a move away from the bases. Without the diagonal, every fold has to be planned individually. There is no shortcut. The artist designs every crease from first principles, usually by working backward from the finished form to a crease pattern that produces it. This is harder. It is also the basis of contemporary origami's most ambitious work — the insects, the architectural models, the deployable structures used in engineering.

The lesson is not that the diagonal is always right. The lesson is that the diagonal is the default, and a default is something you depart from only after you have understood it.

## Looking back

Three chapters in, we have a picture. Folding is geometry made physical (Chapter 1). The geometry organizes itself into bases, and the bases organize themselves into models (Chapter 2). The bases all start at the diagonal because the diagonal gives the most (Chapter 3).

What comes next is technique. From here on, you will be folding. Every chapter from here will end with a model. You will start with the simplest — the cup, the boat, the airplane — and work upward toward the crane, the lily, and beyond. The geometry, the history, and the principle of the diagonal will sit in the background. The paper will be in your hands.
